did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used an auto-diagnosis system for skin cancer classification. Specifically, it employed Improved Local Quinary Pattern (ILQP) for texture feature extraction and a Mixture of Extreme Learning Machine (ELM)-based experts with a trainable gating network (MEETG) as the classifier to distinguish between malignant and benign skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including a proposed method using Color-ILQP and MEETG for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods (preprocessing, feature extraction, classification), describes the datasets used (MED-NOD and Malignant vs. Benign), and reports new experimental results (accuracy, recall, precision) comparing the proposed approach against other methods. Although published in a conference proceedings (IPRIA 2023), it contains original research findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Classification of Skin Cancer With Using Color-ILQP and MEETG
journal: 2023 6th International Conference on Pattern Recognition and Image Analysis (IPRIA)
brief_summary: This study proposes an auto-diagnosis system for skin cancer classification to facilitate the early detection of malignant melanoma. The authors utilize Improved Local Quinary Pattern (ILQP) for texture feature extraction and a Mixture of Extreme Learning Machine-based experts with a trainable gating network (MEETG) as the classifier. The method was evaluated on the MED-NOD and Malignant vs. Benign datasets. The proposed approach achieved classification accuracies of 97.05% on the MED-NOD dataset and 86.61% on the Malignant vs. Benign dataset. These results demonstrate that the MEETG method is more accurate than several compared deep learning models while offering faster training times.

lesion_types_studied.details: Studied as the primary malignant skin cancer class. In the MED-NOD dataset, 70 melanoma images were used. In the Malignant vs. Benign dataset, 1197 malignant images were used for training and 300 for testing.

Studied as the benign counterpart to melanoma. In the MED-NOD dataset, 100 benign moles (naevus) images were used. In the Malignant vs. Benign dataset, 1400 benign images were used for training and 360 for testing. The paper also references 'normal mole' and 'abnormal mole' in the context of related literature (Khalid et.al).

all_task_types.target: Malignant vs. Benign skin lesions (binary classification)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between malignant melanoma and benign nevi (moles). The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories using the MEETG classifier on features extracted via Color-ILQP. Evaluation metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score on the MED-NOD and Malignant vs. Benign datasets.

datasets_used.name: MED-NOD
datasets_used.description: A digital image archive from the Department of Dermatology at the University of Groningen Medical Center, containing images of melanoma and benign moles (naevus).

use.training: 80% of the database images were used for training.
use.testing: 20% of the database images were used for testing.

datasets_used.name: Malignant vs. Benign
datasets_used.description: A balanced dataset of skin mole images taken from the ISIC Archive and available on Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/fanconic/skin-cancer-malignant-vs-benign).

use.training: Images from the 'train' folder were used for training.
use.testing: Images from the 'test' folder were used for testing.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with comparisons made based on these metric values rather than statistical significance.

MED-NOD.notes: The best accuracy on MED-NOD was achieved with 3 experts, 90 hidden neurons, and 8 neighborhoods for ILQP. The proposed method outperformed all compared methods. Accuracy increased by 4.95% compared to method [20] and by 0.55% compared to method [47].

Malignant vs. Benign.notes: The maximum accuracy on the Malignant vs. Benign dataset was 86.61% using 400 hidden neurons, 3 experts, and a combination of P=16 and R=2 features. The proposed method outperformed all compared deep learning models.

architecture_used.main_model: Mixture of Extreme Learning Machine (ELM)-based experts with a trainable gating network (MEETG)

architecture_details.weight_initialization: Randomly initialized input weights and biases for hidden layers; no iterative weight updates required
architecture_details.output_weight_computation: Moore-Penrose generalized inverse (pseudo-inverse) of the hidden layer output matrix
architecture_details.gating_aggregation: Softmax function applied to gate outputs, followed by weighted sum of expert outputs

training_details.training_method: One-pass forward propagation; weights between input and hidden layers are randomly initialized and fixed; output weights are computed analytically
training_details.training_time_note: Significantly faster than back-propagation based models (MLP, DNN) and SVM

classification_or_output_head.output_dimension: Number of classes (2 for binary classification: malignant vs. benign)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Mixture of Extreme Learning Machine (ELM)-based experts with a trainable gating network (MEETG)
RBF SVM (Radial Basis Function Support Vector Machine)
preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using morphological operations: The 'strel' and 'imclose' functions were used to perform morphological dilation and erosion to identify dark hair locations via a generalized grayscale or binary image. The shape of hair pixels was verified as a thin and long structure to remove them from the lesion area.
conclusions: The proposed auto-diagnosis system using Color-ILQP for texture feature extraction and MEETG for classification achieved a classification accuracy of 97.05% on the MED-NOD dataset and 86.61% on the Malignant vs. Benign dataset.
The proposed method outperformed several compared deep learning models and other classification methods on both the MED-NOD and Malignant vs. Benign datasets.
On the MED-NOD dataset, the proposed method's accuracy increased by 4.95% compared to method [20] and by 0.55% compared to method [47].

The MEETG classifier offers significantly faster training times compared to back-propagation based models (such as MLP and DNN) and SVM, while also providing high generalization ability and overcoming issues like local minima and overfitting.
The best performance on the MED-NOD dataset was achieved with 3 experts, 90 hidden neurons, and 8 neighborhoods for ILQP.
The maximum accuracy on the Malignant vs. Benign dataset was achieved with 400 hidden neurons, 3 experts, and a combination of P=16 and R=2 features.

The study demonstrates that computer vision and AI can play a significant role in the early detection of melanoma, potentially reducing mortality by facilitating rapid diagnosis and reducing the need for invasive sampling.
important_remarks: The study relies on two relatively small datasets: MED-NOD (170 images total) and a Kaggle 'Malignant vs. Benign' dataset (3,257 images), which limits the generalizability of the results compared to large-scale clinical trials.

No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the two specific datasets used for training and testing, raising concerns about overfitting and lack of robustness across different populations or imaging devices.

The paper does not report any statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the performance comparisons, making it difficult to assess if the observed improvements over baseline methods are statistically significant.

The preprocessing step for hair removal uses morphological operations which may not be robust to all types of hair density or skin tones, potentially introducing bias or artifacts in real-world clinical settings.

The 'Malignant vs. Benign' dataset is sourced from Kaggle (ISIC Archive), which may contain heterogeneous image qualities and acquisition protocols not representative of a controlled clinical environment.

While the paper claims faster training times compared to deep learning models, it does not provide quantitative benchmarks for inference time or computational resource requirements, which are critical for deployment in clinical workflows.

The study focuses solely on binary classification (malignant vs. benign) and does not address multi-class classification or the differentiation of other skin cancer types (e.g., basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma), limiting its clinical scope.
